case_name: Municipal wastewater reclamation for industrial reuse with fluctuating Alanex-era herbicides, alkylphenol, MTBE, and natural organic matter
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for reclaiming municipal wastewater to industrial reuse quality under uncertain occurrence of Alanex, acetochlor, 4-nonylphenol, MTBE, and variable natural organic matter.

input_data.scenario_background: A peri-urban utility plans to reclaim secondary municipal wastewater for boiler make-up pre-treatment and cooling-related industrial reuse at a nearby manufacturing cluster. Monitoring has intermittently detected Alanex, acetochlor, 4-nonylphenol, and MTBE, but the source contributions are not fully characterised because pesticide-related inflow appears seasonal, illegal discharges are suspected, and storm events dilute some parameters while mobilising others. The utility also expects evolving customer specifications for trace organic removal, so the treatment train must be robust even though the true peak concentrations and co-contaminant mixture are uncertain. Some trace organics are therefore considered suspected but not fully characterised beyond the current screening list.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should be suitable as a reliable industrial reuse feed after site-specific final conditioning, with strong reduction of suspended solids, biodegradable organics, pathogens, natural organic matter, and trace organic contaminants including Alanex, acetochlor, 4-nonylphenol, and MTBE; dissolved salts should be controlled sufficiently for stable downstream industrial operation.
secondary_goals: maintain performance during seasonal and storm-related influent swings
reduce taste-odor and solvent-like MTBE breakthrough risk
limit formation of oxidation by-products where bromide or unknown precursors may be present
provide a train adaptable to tightening trace-organic reuse expectations
avoid overcommitting to a single barrier when contaminant peaks are incompletely defined

core_unit_functions.equalization: buffer hydraulic and water-quality swings and reduce shock loading from seasonal or episodic contaminant inputs
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: remove residual suspended solids and part of high-molecular-weight natural organic matter to reduce downstream oxidant and adsorbent demand
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon_or_ozonation: provide strong polishing of dissolved organics and trace contaminants, with adsorption and biodegradation for alkylphenols and pesticides and improved control of MTBE relative to conventional treatment alone
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: deliver low-solids polishing and protect reverse osmosis from particulate and biological fouling
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reduce dissolved salts and provide an additional barrier for many dissolved constituents to stabilize industrial reuse water quality
core_unit_functions.UV_or_chlorine_based_final_disinfection: control pathogen risk in reclaimed water before delivery

applicability_rationale: a multi-barrier train is most defensible because the source is municipal wastewater with incompletely characterised trace organics and high influent variability
equalization is important because current monitoring indicates intermittent Alanex and acetochlor peaks and uncertain MTBE source behaviour
coagulation and clarification reduce NOM and solids ahead of advanced polishing, improving oxidant efficiency and carbon life
BAC-based polishing is especially suitable when organic micropollutants are mixed and evolving standards may require broader removal than currently listed analytes
RO is justified because industrial reuse users typically require more stable dissolved-solids control than conventional tertiary treatment alone can provide
potential_risks: ozonation effectiveness for MTBE can be limited without sufficient dose and contact time, so the BAC stage remains important
unknown bromide or precursor chemistry could increase by-product risk if oxidation is overapplied under uncertainty
RO concentrate will contain rejected salts and some trace organics, creating disposal and accountability challenges
adsorbent exhaustion or biofilter underperformance may occur if true seasonal pesticide peaks are higher than current screening suggests
this recommendation carries an uncertainty flag because the uncharacterised trace-organic spectrum may alter the preferred oxidation versus adsorption balance

core_unit_functions.equalization: moderate contaminant spikes and variable hydraulic loading
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: remove particulate matter and lower NOM fouling/adsorption competition
core_unit_functions.powdered_or_granular_activated_carbon: adsorb hydrophobic pesticides and 4-nonylphenol and partially reduce broader trace-organic risk
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_advanced_aeration_with_: target volatile MTBE more directly than adsorption alone
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: polish solids and protect downstream membranes
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reduce TDS and provide an additional dissolved-contaminant barrier
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: provide pathogen control before reuse distribution

applicability_rationale: this option explicitly addresses the difficult combination of volatile MTBE and less volatile hydrophobic pesticides/alkylphenol using differentiated barriers
carbon is well aligned with legacy herbicide and nonylphenol concerns, while stripping can improve MTBE control if the compound proves more persistent than initially assumed
it remains robust for industrial reuse because RO provides dissolved-solids stabilization after organic polishing
potential_risks: MTBE stripping is less favorable than for many other VOCs and may require significant air handling, making performance uncertain at low but variable concentrations
off-gas treatment and worker safety controls add complexity
carbon competition from NOM can shorten media life if coagulation is not optimized
RO concentrate and spent carbon both create residuals burdens
this recommendation carries an uncertainty flag because the actual MTBE partitioning behavior and peak concentration profile have not been fully verified

core_unit_functions.equalization: stabilize variable influent quality and loading to the biological system
core_unit_functions.membrane_bioreactor: remove biodegradable organics, suspended solids, and some ammonia while producing a low-turbidity effluent
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: polish residual dissolved organics and adsorb hydrophobic trace organics that pass the bioreactor
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: reduce dissolved salts and add a further barrier for dissolved contaminants
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in reclaimed water

applicability_rationale: MBR offers compact, high-quality biological polishing and can help manage variable municipal wastewater characteristics before advanced polishing
the train is operationally attractive where footprint is limited and a consistently low-solids effluent is needed for downstream membrane reliability
GAC after MBR is a practical hedge against evolving standards for pesticides and alkylphenols
potential_risks: MBR alone is not dependable for removing MTBE and many trace organics to reuse-driven confidence levels
membrane fouling sensitivity may increase during storm-related variability unless equalization is effectively sized
GAC exhaustion could accelerate if NOM remains elevated
NF may be insufficient if the end user later demands tighter salinity control, in which case RO would be required
this recommendation carries an uncertainty flag because biological attenuation of the specific herbicide mixture may be inconsistent across seasons

core_unit_functions.equalization: dampen hydraulic surges before the natural treatment stage
core_unit_functions.constructed_wetland_or_engineered_natura: provide sedimentation, plant uptake, bacterial degradation, and chemical adsorption for wastewater polishing
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: remove residual solids and improve clarity
core_unit_functions.activated_carbon: provide final adsorption polishing for residual trace organics
core_unit_functions.disinfection: control pathogen risk for nonpotable industrial reuse

applicability_rationale: engineered natural systems are relevant because the source is municipal wastewater and can reduce common pollutants while adding resilience benefits
this route can be attractive where energy minimization and ecological co-benefits are valued
the combination with carbon polishing acknowledges that natural systems alone are unlikely to satisfy confidence needs for the listed micropollutants
potential_risks: trace-organic removal is less predictable for Alanex, acetochlor, and especially MTBE than in more intensive advanced-treatment trains
land area and seasonal performance may be limiting despite a nominally medium footprint constraint
dissolved salts remain largely untreated, which may be problematic for some industrial users
wet-weather short-circuiting or temperature effects could reduce treatment reliability
this recommendation carries an uncertainty flag because evolving industrial reuse standards may outpace what a natural-system-led train can reliably demonstrate

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: destabilize colloids and remove some particulate-associated organics
core_unit_functions.sedimentation: separate formed flocs and settled solids

applicability_rationale: included as a lower-ranked conventional baseline option

it can improve turbidity, solids, and part of bulk organic load, but it does not provide a credible high-confidence barrier for the listed trace organics or dissolved salts needed for industrial reuse stability
potential_risks: insufficient removal of MTBE, pesticides, and 4-nonylphenol for a risk-aware industrial reuse program
little control of TDS and hardness variability
NOM may remain high enough to interfere with downstream industrial processes or later disinfection choices
this recommendation carries a strong uncertainty warning because it relies on unproven incidental removal of contaminants that are only partially characterised
